Alongside many [domestic obligations](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_military_operations#Domestic "List of Canadian military operations"), more than 3,000 [Canadian Armed Forces](/wiki/Canadian_Armed_Forces "Canadian Armed Forces") (CAF) personnel are [deployed in multiple foreign military operations](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_military_operations#Foreign "List of Canadian military operations").[[217]](#cite_note-220) The Canadian unified forces comprise the [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy"), [Canadian Army](/wiki/Canadian_Army "Canadian Army"), and [Royal Canadian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Air_Force "Royal Canadian Air Force"). The nation employs a professional, volunteer force of approximately 68,000 active personnel and 27,000 reserve personnel—increasing to 71,500 and 30,000 respectively under "Strong, Secure, Engaged"[[218]](#cite_note-221)—with a sub-component of approximately 5,000 [Canadian Rangers](/wiki/Canadian_Rangers "Canadian Rangers").[[219]](#cite_note-222)[[d]](#cite_note-Force-224) In 2022, Canada's military expenditure totalled approximately $26.9 billion, or around 1.2 percent of the country's [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP) – placing it 14th for [military expenditure by country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_military_expenditures "List of countries by military expenditures").[[221]](#cite_note-SIPRI-2022-225)

[Canada's role in developing *peacekeeping* and its participation in major peacekeeping initiatives](/wiki/Canadian_peacekeeping "Canadian peacekeeping") during the 20th century has played a major role in its positive global image.[[222]](#cite_note-226) Peacekeeping is deeply embedded in Canadian culture and a distinguishing feature that Canadians feel sets their foreign policy apart from the United States.[[223]](#cite_note-227) Canada has long been reluctant to participate in military operations that are not sanctioned by the United Nations,[[224]](#cite_note-Massie_2019_pp._575–594-228) such as the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Canada_and_the_Vietnam_War "Canada and the Vietnam War") or the [2003 invasion of Iraq](/wiki/Canada_and_the_Iraq_War "Canada and the Iraq War").[[224]](#cite_note-Massie_2019_pp._575–594-228) Since the 21st century, Canadian direct participation in UN peacekeeping efforts has greatly declined.[[225]](#cite_note-JohnsonJoshee2007-229) The large decrease was a result of Canada directing its participation to UN-sanctioned military [operations through the North Atlantic Treaty Organization](/wiki/Canada_in_NATO "Canada in NATO"), rather than directly through the UN.[[226]](#cite_note-McQuaig2010bt-230) The change to participation via NATO has resulted in a shift towards more militarized and deadly missions rather than traditional peacekeeping duties.[[227]](#cite_note-James_Michaud_OReilly_2006_p._177-231)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Canada](/wiki/Economy_of_Canada "Economy of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Toronto_from_above_at_night.jpg/250px-Toronto_from_above_at_night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_from_above_at_night.jpg)

The [Toronto financial district](/wiki/Financial_District,_Toronto "Financial District, Toronto") is the second-largest financial centre in North America, the seventh-largest globally in employment and the heart of Canada's finance industry.[[228]](#cite_note-232)

Canada's [mixed-market economy](/wiki/Mixed-market "Mixed-market")[[229]](#cite_note-233) is highly [developed](/wiki/Developed_economy "Developed economy"), ranking as the world's [ninth-largest by nominal GDP](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)") as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Canada&action=edit), at approximately [US$](/wiki/United_States_dollar "United States dollar")2.221 trillion.[[230]](#cite_note-234) The country is one of the world's largest [trading nations](/wiki/Trading_nation "Trading nation"), with a highly [globalized](/wiki/Globalized "Globalized") economy.[[231]](#cite_note-235) In 2021, Canadian trade in goods and services reached $2.016 trillion.[[232]](#cite_note-econ-236) [Canada's exports](/wiki/Canada%27s_exports "Canada's exports") totalled over $637 billion, while its imported goods were worth over $631 billion, of which approximately $391 billion originated from the United States.[[232]](#cite_note-econ-236) In 2018, Canada had a [trade deficit](/wiki/Trade_deficit "Trade deficit") in goods of $22 billion and a trade deficit in services of $25 billion.[[232]](#cite_note-econ-236) The [Toronto Stock Exchange](/wiki/Toronto_Stock_Exchange "Toronto Stock Exchange") is the ninth-largest stock exchange in the world by [market capitalization](/wiki/Market_capitalization "Market capitalization"), listing over 1,500 companies with a combined market capitalization of over US$2 trillion.[[233]](#cite_note-237)

The [Bank of Canada](/wiki/Bank_of_Canada "Bank of Canada") is the [central bank](/wiki/Central_bank "Central bank") of the country.[[234]](#cite_note-Watts-1993-238) The [minister of finance](/wiki/Minister_of_Finance_(Canada) "Minister of Finance (Canada)") and [minister of innovation, science, and industry](/wiki/Minister_of_Innovation,_Science_and_Industry "Minister of Innovation, Science and Industry") use data from [Statistics Canada](/wiki/Statistics_Canada "Statistics Canada") to enable financial planning and develop economic policy.[[235]](#cite_note-239) Canada has a strong [cooperative banking](/wiki/Cooperative_banking "Cooperative banking") sector, with the world's highest per-capita membership in [credit unions](/wiki/Credit_union "Credit union").[[236]](#cite_note-240) It ranks low in the [Corruption Perceptions Index](/wiki/Corruption_Perceptions_Index "Corruption Perceptions Index") (14th in 2023)[[237]](#cite_note-cpi-241) and "is widely regarded as among the least corrupt countries of the world".[[238]](#cite_note-242) It ranks high in the Global Competitiveness Report (19th in 2024).[[239]](#cite_note-x737-243) Canada's economy ranks above most [Western nations](/wiki/Western_nations "Western nations") on [the Heritage Foundation](/wiki/The_Heritage_Foundation "The Heritage Foundation")'s [Index of Economic Freedom](/wiki/Index_of_Economic_Freedom "Index of Economic Freedom")[[240]](#cite_note-244) and experiences a relatively low level of [income disparity](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality").[[241]](#cite_note-245) The country's average household [disposable income](/wiki/Disposable_income "Disposable income") per capita is "well above" the OECD average.[[242]](#cite_note-246) Canada ranks among the lowest of the most developed countries for [housing affordability](/wiki/Affordable_housing_in_Canada "Affordable housing in Canada")[[243]](#cite_note-247) and [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment").[[244]](#cite_note-248) The [unemployment rate](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_provinces_by_unemployment_rate "List of Canadian provinces by unemployment rate") in 2025 was 6.8 percent.[[245]](#cite_note-249)

Since the early 20th century, the growth of [Canada's manufacturing](/wiki/Manufacturing_in_Canada "Manufacturing in Canada"), mining, and service sectors has transformed the nation from a largely rural economy to an urbanized, industrial one.[[246]](#cite_note-250) The Canadian economy is dominated by the [service industry](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy"), which employs about three-quarters of the country's workforce.[[247]](#cite_note-251) Canada has an unusually important [primary sector](/wiki/Primary_sector_of_the_economy "Primary sector of the economy"), of which the [forestry](/wiki/Forestry_in_Canada "Forestry in Canada") and [petroleum industries](/wiki/Petroleum_industry_in_Canada "Petroleum industry in Canada") are the most prominent components.[[248]](#cite_note-SueyoshiGoto2018-252) Many towns in northern Canada, where agriculture is difficult, are sustained by nearby mines or sources of timber.[[249]](#cite_note-253)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/FTAs_with_Canada.svg/330px-FTAs_with_Canada.svg.png)](/wiki/File:FTAs_with_Canada.svg)

Canada

[Countries and territories with free-trade agreements](/wiki/Free_trade_agreements_of_Canada "Free trade agreements of Canada")[[250]](#cite_note-GAC_2020-254)

Canada's economic integration with the United States has increased significantly since the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War").[[251]](#cite_note-255) The [Canada – United States Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/Canada_%E2%80%93_United_States_Free_Trade_Agreement "Canada – United States Free Trade Agreement") (FTA) of 1988 eliminated tariffs between the two countries, while the [North American Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/North_American_Free_Trade_Agreement "North American Free Trade Agreement") (NAFTA) expanded the free-trade zone to include [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") in 1994 (later replaced by the [Canada–United States–Mexico Agreement](/wiki/United_States%E2%80%93Mexico%E2%80%93Canada_Agreement "United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement")).[[252]](#cite_note-256) As of 2023, [Canada is a signatory to 15 free trade agreements](/wiki/Free_trade_agreements_of_Canada "Free trade agreements of Canada") with 51 different countries.[[250]](#cite_note-GAC_2020-254)

Canada is one of the few developed nations that are net exporters of energy.[[253]](#cite_note-257) [Atlantic Canada possess vast offshore deposits of natural gas](/wiki/Offshore_drilling_in_Atlantic_Canada "Offshore drilling in Atlantic Canada"),[[254]](#cite_note-258) and Alberta hosts the fourth-largest oil reserves in the world.[[255]](#cite_note-Monga_2022-259) The vast [Athabasca oil sands](/wiki/Athabasca_oil_sands "Athabasca oil sands") and other oil reserves give Canada 13 percent of global oil reserves, constituting the world's [third- or fourth-largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_proven_oil_reserves "List of countries by proven oil reserves").[[256]](#cite_note-260) Canada is additionally one of the world's [largest suppliers of agricultural products](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Canada "Agriculture in Canada"); the Canadian Prairies region is one of the most important global producers of wheat, [canola](/wiki/Canola "Canola"), and other grains.[[257]](#cite_note-261) [Canada's main exports](/wiki/List_of_exports_of_Canada "List of exports of Canada") are zinc, uranium, gold, nickel, [platinoids](/wiki/Platinum_group "Platinum group"), aluminum, steel, iron ore, coking coal, lead, copper, [molybdenum](/wiki/Molybdenum "Molybdenum"), cobalt, and cadmium.[[258]](#cite_note-262) Canada has a sizeable manufacturing sector centred in southern Ontario and Quebec, with automobiles and [aeronautics](/wiki/Aeronautics "Aeronautics") representing particularly important industries.[[259]](#cite_note-263) The [country's fishing](/wiki/Fishing_industry_in_Canada "Fishing industry in Canada") and [tourism industries](/wiki/Tourism_in_Canada "Tourism in Canada") are also a key contributor to the economy.[[260]](#cite_note-264)

### Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in Canada](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Canada "Science and technology in Canada")

In 2020, Canada spent approximately $41.9 billion on domestic [research and development](/wiki/Research_and_development "Research and development"), with supplementary estimates for 2022 at $43.2 billion.[[261]](#cite_note-265) As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Canada&action=edit), the country has produced 15 [Nobel laureates](/wiki/List_of_Nobel_laureates_by_country "List of Nobel laureates by country") in [physics](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physics "Nobel Prize in Physics"), [chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"), and [medicine](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Physiology_or_Medicine "Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine").[[262]](#cite_note-266) The country [ranks seventh](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_scientific_and_technical_journal_articles "List of countries by number of scientific and technical journal articles") in the worldwide share of articles published in [scientific journals](/wiki/Scientific_journals "Scientific journals"), according to the [Nature Index](/wiki/Nature_Index "Nature Index"),[[263]](#cite_note-267) and is home to the headquarters of a number of global technology firms.[[264]](#cite_note-268) Canada [has one of the highest levels of Internet access in the world](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_number_of_Internet_users "List of countries by number of Internet users"), with approximately 95 percent of its population aged 15 and above.[[265]](#cite_note-g345-269)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2d/STS-116_-_P5_Truss_hand-off_to_ISS_%28NASA_S116-E-05765%29.jpg/250px-STS-116_-_P5_Truss_hand-off_to_ISS_%28NASA_S116-E-05765%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:STS-116_-_P5_Truss_hand-off_to_ISS_(NASA_S116-E-05765).jpg)

The Canadian-built [Space Shuttle](/wiki/Space_Shuttle "Space Shuttle") robotic arm (left), referred to as [Canadarm](/wiki/Canadarm "Canadarm"), transferred the [P5 truss segment](/wiki/Integrated_Truss_Structure "Integrated Truss Structure") over to the Canadian-built [space station](/wiki/Space_station "Space station") robotic arm, referred to as [Canadarm2](/wiki/Canadarm2 "Canadarm2").[[266]](#cite_note-Canadian_Space_Agency_2002_a865-270)

[Canada's achievements in science and technology](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_inventions,_innovations,_and_discoveries "List of Canadian inventions, innovations, and discoveries") include the creation of the modern [alkaline battery](/wiki/Alkaline_battery "Alkaline battery"),[[267]](#cite_note-271) the [discovery of insulin](/wiki/Insulin#Discovery "Insulin"),[[268]](#cite_note-272) the development of the [polio vaccine](/wiki/Polio_vaccine "Polio vaccine"),[[269]](#cite_note-273) and discoveries about the interior structure of the [atomic nucleus](/wiki/Atomic_nucleus "Atomic nucleus").[[270]](#cite_note-274) Other major Canadian scientific contributions include the [artificial cardiac pacemaker](/wiki/Artificial_cardiac_pacemaker "Artificial cardiac pacemaker"), mapping the [visual cortex](/wiki/Visual_cortex "Visual cortex"),[[271]](#cite_note-275) the development of the [electron microscope](/wiki/Electron_microscope "Electron microscope"),[[272]](#cite_note-276) [plate tectonics](/wiki/Plate_tectonics "Plate tectonics"), [deep learning](/wiki/Deep_learning "Deep learning"), [multi-touch](/wiki/Multi-touch "Multi-touch") technology, and the identification of the first [black hole](/wiki/Black_hole "Black hole"), [Cygnus X-1](/wiki/Cygnus_X-1 "Cygnus X-1").[[273]](#cite_note-277) Canada has a long history of discovery in genetics, which include [stem cells](/wiki/Stem_cell "Stem cell"), [site-directed mutagenesis](/wiki/Site-directed_mutagenesis "Site-directed mutagenesis"), [T-cell receptor](/wiki/T-cell_receptor "T-cell receptor"), and the identification of the genes that cause [Fanconi anemia](/wiki/Fanconi_anemia "Fanconi anemia"), [cystic fibrosis](/wiki/Cystic_fibrosis "Cystic fibrosis"), and [early-onset Alzheimer's disease](/wiki/Early-onset_Alzheimer%27s_disease "Early-onset Alzheimer's disease"), among numerous other diseases.[[274]](#cite_note-278)

The [Canadian Space Agency](/wiki/Canadian_Space_Agency "Canadian Space Agency") runs an active space program focused on deep-space, planetary, and aviation research, along with rockets and satellites.[[275]](#cite_note-279) Canada launched its first satellite, [Alouette 1](/wiki/Alouette_1 "Alouette 1"), in 1962.[[276]](#cite_note-280) It contributes to the [International Space Station](/wiki/International_Space_Station "International Space Station") and is known for its robotic tools, such as multiple [Canadarms](/wiki/Canadarm "Canadarm").[[277]](#cite_note-281) Canada has initiated many long-term projects, including the [Radarsat satellite series](/wiki/Radarsat "Radarsat") and the [Black Brant rocket series](/wiki/Black_Brant_(rocket) "Black Brant (rocket)").[[278]](#cite_note-282)

## Demographics

Main articles: [Demographics of Canada](/wiki/Demographics_of_Canada "Demographics of Canada"), [List of cities in Canada](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Canada "List of cities in Canada"), and [List of census metropolitan areas and agglomerations in Canada](/wiki/List_of_census_metropolitan_areas_and_agglomerations_in_Canada "List of census metropolitan areas and agglomerations in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Population_density_statistics_canada.gif/330px-Population_density_statistics_canada.gif)](/wiki/File:Population_density_statistics_canada.gif)

[Canada population](/wiki/Population_of_Canada "Population of Canada") density map (2014)  
Top left: The [Quebec City–Windsor Corridor](/wiki/Quebec_City%E2%80%93Windsor_Corridor "Quebec City–Windsor Corridor") is the most densely inhabited and heavily industrialized region.[[279]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2015_l621-283)

The [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") enumerated [a total population](/wiki/Population_of_Canada_by_year "Population of Canada by year") of 36,991,981, an increase of around 5.2 percent over the 2016 figure.[[280]](#cite_note-2021cen-284) It is estimated that Canada's population surpassed 40,000,000 in 2023.[[281]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2023_e538-285) The main drivers of population growth are [immigration](/wiki/Immigration_to_Canada "Immigration to Canada") and, to a lesser extent, natural growth.[[282]](#cite_note-286) Canada has one of the highest per-capita immigration rates in the world,[[283]](#cite_note-287) driven mainly by [economic policy](/wiki/Economic_impact_of_immigration_to_Canada "Economic impact of immigration to Canada") and [family reunification](/wiki/Immigration_categories_(Canada) "Immigration categories (Canada)").[[284]](#cite_note-288) A record 483,390 immigrants were admitted in 2024.[[285]](#cite_note-289) Canada leads the world in [refugee resettlement](/wiki/Third_country_resettlement "Third country resettlement"); it resettled more than 47,600 in 2022.[[286]](#cite_note-g225-290) New immigrants settle mostly in [major urban areas](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_population_centres_in_Canada "List of the largest population centres in Canada"), such as Toronto, Montreal, and Vancouver.[[287]](#cite_note-291)

Canada's population density, at 4.2 inhabitants per square kilometre (11/sq mi), is among the lowest in the world,[[280]](#cite_note-2021cen-284) with approximately 95 percent of the population residing south of the [55th parallel](/wiki/55th_parallel_north "55th parallel north") north.[[288]](#cite_note-OECD2014-292) About 80 percent of the population lives within 150 kilometres (93 mi) of the border with the contiguous United States.[[289]](#cite_note-293) Canada is highly urbanized, with over 80 percent of the population living in urban centres.[[290]](#cite_note-World_Bank_Open_Data_s787-294) The majority of Canadians (over 70 percent ) live below the [49th parallel](/wiki/49th_parallel_north "49th parallel north"), with 50 percent of Canadians living south of 45°42′ (45.7 degrees) north.[[291]](#cite_note-a715-295) The most densely populated part of the country is the [Quebec City–Windsor Corridor](/wiki/Quebec_City%E2%80%93Windsor_Corridor "Quebec City–Windsor Corridor") in Southern Quebec and Southern Ontario along the Great Lakes and the St. Lawrence River.[[292]](#cite_note-McMurryShepherd2004-296)

The majority of Canadians (81.1 percent) live in family households, 12.1 percent report living alone, and 6.8 percent live with other relatives or unrelated persons.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-297) Fifty-one percent of households are couples with or without children, 8.7 percent are single-parent households, 2.9 percent are multigenerational households, and 29.3 percent are single-person households.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-297)

### Ethnicity

Main article: [Ethnic origins of people in Canada](/wiki/Ethnic_origins_of_people_in_Canada "Ethnic origins of people in Canada")

Respondents in the [2021 Canadian census](/wiki/2021_Canadian_census "2021 Canadian census") self-reported over 450 "[ethnic or cultural origins](/wiki/Ethnic_origins_of_people_in_Canada "Ethnic origins of people in Canada")".[[295]](#cite_note-2021newsrelease-299) The major [panethnic](/wiki/Panethnicity "Panethnicity") groups chosen were: [European](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Europe "Ethnic groups in Europe") (52.5 percent), [North American](/wiki/North_America "North America") (22.9 percent), [Asian](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_Asia "Ethnic groups in Asia") (19.3 percent), [North American Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Indigenous peoples of the Americas") (6.1 percent), [African](/wiki/List_of_ethnic_groups_of_Africa "List of ethnic groups of Africa") (3.8 percent), [Latin, Central and South American](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America") (2.5 percent), [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean_people "Caribbean people") (2.1 percent), [Oceanian](/wiki/Oceanian_people "Oceanian people") (0.3 percent), and other (6 percent).[[296]](#cite_note-300) Over 60 percent of Canadians reported a single origin, and 36 percent reported having multiple ethnic origins, thus the overall total is greater than 100 percent.[[295]](#cite_note-2021newsrelease-299)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Canadian_ethnocultural_diversity.png/330px-Canadian_ethnocultural_diversity.png)](/wiki/File:Canadian_ethnocultural_diversity.png)

The top 168 [ethnic or cultural origins self-reported by Canadians](/wiki/Ethnic_origins_of_people_in_Canada "Ethnic origins of people in Canada") in the 2021 census[[297]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_u055-301)

The country's ten largest self-reported ethnic or cultural origins in 2021 were [Canadian](/wiki/Canadian_ethnicity "Canadian ethnicity")[[e]](#cite_note-Canadian-304) (accounting for 15.6 percent of the population), followed by [English](/wiki/English_Canadians "English Canadians") (14.7 percent), [Irish](/wiki/Irish_Canadians "Irish Canadians") (12.1 percent), [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_Canadians "Scottish Canadians") (12.1 percent), [French](/wiki/French_Canadians "French Canadians") (11.0 percent), [German](/wiki/German_Canadians "German Canadians") (8.1 percent), [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_Canadians "Chinese Canadians") (4.7 percent), [Italian](/wiki/Italian_Canadians "Italian Canadians") (4.3 percent), [Indian](/wiki/Indian_Canadians "Indian Canadians") (3.7 percent), and [Ukrainian](/wiki/Ukrainian_Canadians "Ukrainian Canadians") (3.5 percent).[[300]](#cite_note-ethnicpopulationordered2021-305)

Of the 36.3 million people enumerated in 2021, approximately 25.4 million reported being "[White](/wiki/White_people "White people")", representing 69.8 percent of the population.[[295]](#cite_note-2021newsrelease-299) The Indigenous population representing 5 percent or 1.8 million people, grew by 9.4 percent compared to the non-Indigenous population, which grew by 5.3 percent from 2016 to 2021.[[301]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2022a-306) One out of every four Canadians or 26.5 percent of the population belonged to a non-White and non-Indigenous [visible minority](/wiki/Visible_minority "Visible minority"),[[302]](#cite_note-307)[[f]](#cite_note-309) the largest of which in 2021 were [South Asian](/wiki/South_Asian_Canadians "South Asian Canadians") (2.6 million people; 7.1 percent), Chinese (1.7 million; 4.7 percent), [Black](/wiki/Black_Canadians "Black Canadians") (1.5 million; 4.3 percent), [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipino_Canadians "Filipino Canadians") (960,000 2.6 percent), [Arabs](/wiki/Arab_Canadians "Arab Canadians") (690,000; 1.9 percent), Latin Americans (580,000; 1.6 percent), [Southeast Asians](/wiki/Southeast_Asian_Canadians "Southeast Asian Canadians") (390,000; 1.1 percent), [West Asians](/wiki/West_Asian_Canadians "West Asian Canadians") (360,000; 1.0 percent), [Koreans](/wiki/Korean_Canadians "Korean Canadians") (220,000; 0.6 percent) and [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_Canadians "Japanese Canadians") (99,000; 0.3 percent).[[295]](#cite_note-2021newsrelease-299)

Between 2011 and 2016, the visible minority population rose by 18.4 percent.[[304]](#cite_note-310) In 1961, about 300,000 people, less than two percent of Canada's population, were members of visible minority groups.[[305]](#cite_note-311) The 2021 census indicated that 8.3 million people, or almost one-quarter (23.0 percent) of the population, reported themselves as being or having been a [landed immigrant or permanent resident](/wiki/Permanent_residency_in_Canada "Permanent residency in Canada") in Canada—above the [1921 census](/wiki/1921_Canadian_census "1921 Canadian census") previous record of 22.3 percent.[[306]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2022b-312) In 2021, India, China, and the Philippines were the top three countries of origin for immigrants moving to Canada.[[307]](#cite_note-313)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of Canada](/wiki/Languages_of_Canada "Languages of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/English-French_bilingualism_rate_in_Canada.png/330px-English-French_bilingualism_rate_in_Canada.png)](/wiki/File:English-French_bilingualism_rate_in_Canada.png)

Percentage of the population who could conduct a conversation in English and French in 2021[[308]](#cite_note-f185-314)

A multitude of languages are used by Canadians, with [English](/wiki/Canadian_English "Canadian English") and [French](/wiki/Canadian_French "Canadian French") (the [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language")) being the [mother tongues](/wiki/First_language "First language") of approximately 54 percent and 19 percent of Canadians, respectively.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-297) [Canada's official bilingualism policies](/wiki/Official_bilingualism_in_Canada "Official bilingualism in Canada") give citizens the right to receive federal government services in either English or French with official-[language minorities](/wiki/Minority_language "Minority language") guaranteed their own schools in all provinces and territories.[[309]](#cite_note-315)

Quebec's 1974 *[Official Language Act](/wiki/Official_Language_Act_(Quebec) "Official Language Act (Quebec)")* established French as the only official language of the province.[[310]](#cite_note-316) Although more than 82 percent of French-speaking Canadians live in Quebec, there are substantial [Francophone](/wiki/Francophone "Francophone") populations in [New Brunswick](/wiki/Demographics_of_New_Brunswick "Demographics of New Brunswick"), [Alberta](/wiki/Franco-Albertans "Franco-Albertans"), and [Manitoba](/wiki/Franco-Manitoban "Franco-Manitoban"), with [Ontario](/wiki/Franco-Ontarian "Franco-Ontarian") having the largest French-speaking population outside Quebec.[[311]](#cite_note-317) New Brunswick, the only officially bilingual province, has an [Acadian French](/wiki/Acadian_French "Acadian French") minority constituting 33 percent of the population.[[312]](#cite_note-318) There are also clusters of Acadians in southwestern Nova Scotia, on Cape Breton Island, and in central and western Prince Edward Island.[[313]](#cite_note-319)

Other provinces have no official languages as such, but French is used as a language of instruction, in courts, and for other government services, in addition to English. Manitoba, Ontario, and Quebec allow for both English and French to be spoken in the provincial legislatures and laws are enacted in both languages. In Ontario, French has some legal status, but is not fully co-official.[[314]](#cite_note-320) There are 11 [Indigenous language groups](/wiki/Languages_of_Canada "Languages of Canada"), composed of more than 65 distinct languages and dialects.[[315]](#cite_note-321) Several Indigenous languages have official status in the Northwest Territories.[[316]](#cite_note-322) [Inuktitut](/wiki/Inuktitut "Inuktitut") is the majority language in Nunavut and is one of three official languages in the territory.[[317]](#cite_note-323)

As of the 2021 census, just over 7.8 million Canadians listed a non-official language as their [first language](/wiki/First_language "First language"). Some of the most common non-official first languages include [Mandarin](/wiki/Mandarin_Chinese "Mandarin Chinese") (679,255 first-language speakers), [Punjabi](/wiki/Punjabi_language "Punjabi language") (666,585), [Cantonese](/wiki/Cantonese "Cantonese") (553,380), Spanish (538,870), [Arabic](/wiki/Arabic "Arabic") (508,410), [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language") (461,150), Italian (319,505), German (272,865), and [Tamil](/wiki/Tamil_language "Tamil language") (237,890).[[293]](#cite_note-:0-297) The country is also home to many [sign languages](/wiki/Sign_language "Sign language"), some of which are Indigenous.[[318]](#cite_note-324) [American Sign Language](/wiki/American_Sign_Language "American Sign Language") (ASL) is used across the country due to the prevalence of ASL in primary and secondary schools.[[319]](#cite_note-325) [Quebec Sign Language](/wiki/Quebec_Sign_Language "Quebec Sign Language") (LSQ) is used primarily in Quebec.[[320]](#cite_note-326)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Canada](/wiki/Religion_in_Canada "Religion in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Freedom_of_Religion_%2838007857792%29.jpg/250px-Freedom_of_Religion_%2838007857792%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Freedom_of_Religion_(38007857792).jpg)

[Freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Canada "Freedom of religion in Canada") sculpture by Marlene Hilton Moore at the McMurtry Gardens of Justice in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")[[321]](#cite_note-McMurtry_Gardens_of_Justice-327)

Canada is religiously diverse, encompassing a wide range of beliefs and customs.[[322]](#cite_note-Cornelissen_2021-328) The [Constitution of Canada](/wiki/Constitution_of_Canada "Constitution of Canada") refers to God; however, Canada has no official church and the government is officially committed to [religious pluralism](/wiki/Religious_pluralism "Religious pluralism").[[323]](#cite_note-329) [Freedom of religion in Canada](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Canada "Freedom of religion in Canada") is a constitutionally protected right.[[324]](#cite_note-330)

Rates of religious adherence have steadily decreased since the 1970s.[[322]](#cite_note-Cornelissen_2021-328) With Christianity in decline after having once been central and integral to Canadian culture and daily life,[[325]](#cite_note-Roberts2005w-331) Canada has become a [post-Christian](/wiki/Postchristianity "Postchristianity"), [secular](/wiki/Secular "Secular") state.[[326]](#cite_note-332) Although the majority of Canadians consider [religion to be unimportant](/wiki/Importance_of_religion_by_country "Importance of religion by country") in their daily lives,[[327]](#cite_note-Punnett2015-333) they still believe in God.[[328]](#cite_note-Haskell2009-334) The practice of religion is generally considered a private matter.[[329]](#cite_note-BoyleSheen2013-335)

According to the 2021 census, [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Canada "Christianity in Canada") is the largest religion in Canada, with [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") representing 29.9 percent of the population having the most adherents. [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") overall representing 53.3 percent of the population,[[g]](#cite_note-religion-338) are followed by people reporting [irreligion or having no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Canada "Irreligion in Canada") at 34.6 percent. Other faiths include [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Canada "Islam in Canada") (4.9 percent), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Canada "Hinduism in Canada") (2.3 percent), [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism_in_Canada "Sikhism in Canada") (2.1 percent), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Canada "Buddhism in Canada") (1.0 percent), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism_in_Canada "Judaism in Canada") (0.9 percent), and [Indigenous spirituality](/wiki/Mythologies_of_the_indigenous_peoples_of_the_Americas "Mythologies of the indigenous peoples of the Americas") (0.2 percent).[[330]](#cite_note-religion2021-336) Canada has the [second-largest national Sikh population](/wiki/Sikhism_by_country "Sikhism by country"), behind [India](/wiki/Sikhism_in_India "Sikhism in India").[[332]](#cite_note-339)

### Health

Main article: [Healthcare in Canada](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Canada "Healthcare in Canada")

Healthcare in Canada is delivered through the provincial and territorial systems of [publicly funded health care](/wiki/Publicly_funded_health_care "Publicly funded health care"), informally called [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(Canada) "Medicare (Canada)").[[333]](#cite_note-340) It is guided by the provisions of the *[Canada Health Act](/wiki/Canada_Health_Act "Canada Health Act")* of 1984[[334]](#cite_note-341) and is [universal](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care").[[335]](#cite_note-342) Universal access to publicly funded health services "is often considered by Canadians as a fundamental value that ensures national healthcare insurance for everyone wherever they live in the country".[[336]](#cite_note-343) Around 30 percent of Canadians' healthcare is paid for through the private sector.[[337]](#cite_note-Kroll2012-344) This mostly pays for services not covered or partially covered by Medicare, such as [prescription drugs](/wiki/Prescription_drug "Prescription drug"), [dentistry](/wiki/Dentistry "Dentistry") and [optometry](/wiki/Optometry "Optometry").[[337]](#cite_note-Kroll2012-344) Approximately 65 to 75 percent of Canadians have some form of supplementary health insurance; many receive it through their employers or access secondary social service programs.[[338]](#cite_note-345)

[![graph of expenditures as described in the caption](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/OECD_health_expenditure_per_capita_by_country.svg/330px-OECD_health_expenditure_per_capita_by_country.svg.png)](/wiki/File:OECD_health_expenditure_per_capita_by_country.svg)

[Health expenditure and financing by country](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_total_health_expenditure_per_capita "List of countries by total health expenditure per capita"). Total health expenditure per capita in US dollars (PPP).

In common with many other developed countries, Canada is experiencing an increase in healthcare expenditures due to a [demographic shift](/wiki/Demographic_transition "Demographic transition") toward an older population, with more retirees and fewer people of working age. In 2021, the average age in Canada was 41.9 years.[[293]](#cite_note-:0-297) Life expectancy is 81.1 years.[[339]](#cite_note-346) A 2016 report by the [chief public health officer](/wiki/Chief_Public_Health_Officer_of_Canada "Chief Public Health Officer of Canada") found that 88 percent of Canadians, one of the highest proportions of the population among G7 countries, indicated that they "had good or very good health".[[340]](#cite_note-347) Eighty percent of Canadian adults self-report having at least one major risk factor for chronic disease: smoking, physical inactivity, unhealthy eating or excessive alcohol use.[[341]](#cite_note-GregoryStephens2019-348) Canada has one of the highest rates of adult obesity among [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") countries, contributing to approximately 2.7 million cases of [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes").[[341]](#cite_note-GregoryStephens2019-348) Four chronic diseases—[cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") (leading cause of death), [cardiovascular diseases](/wiki/Cardiovascular_diseases "Cardiovascular diseases"), [respiratory diseases](/wiki/Respiratory_diseases "Respiratory diseases"), and diabetes—account for 65 percent of deaths in Canada.[[342]](#cite_note-349) There are approximately 8 million people aged 15 and older with one or more [disabilities in Canada](/wiki/Disability_in_Canada "Disability in Canada").[[343]](#cite_note-k471-350)

In 2024, the [Canadian Institute for Health Information](/wiki/Canadian_Institute_for_Health_Information "Canadian Institute for Health Information") estimated that healthcare spending reached $372 billion, or 12.4 percent of Canada's GDP for that year.[[344]](#cite_note-351) In 2022, Canada's per-capita spending on health expenditures ranked 12th among [health-care systems in the OECD](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_total_health_expenditure_per_capita "List of countries by total health expenditure per capita").[[345]](#cite_note-theOECD_h799-352) Canada has performed close to, or above the average on the majority of OECD health indicators since the early 2000s, ranking above the average on OECD indicators for wait-times and access to care, with average scores for quality of care and use of resources.[[346]](#cite_note-353) The [Commonwealth Fund's](/wiki/Commonwealth_Fund "Commonwealth Fund") 2021 report comparing the healthcare systems of the 11 most [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_countries "Developed countries") ranked Canada second-to-last.[[347]](#cite_note-commonwealthfund-354) Identified weaknesses were comparatively higher infant mortality rate, the prevalence of chronic conditions, long wait times, poor availability of after-hours care, and a lack of prescription drugs and dental coverage.[[347]](#cite_note-commonwealthfund-354) An increasing problem in Canada's health system is a lack of healthcare professionals,[[348]](#cite_note-Duong_Vogel_2023_pp._E309–E310-355) and hospital capacity.[[349]](#cite_note-Thiessen_Partner_Organization_Ma_2023_j399-356)

### Education

Main articles: [Education in Canada](/wiki/Education_in_Canada "Education in Canada") and [Higher education in Canada](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Canada "Higher education in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d9/Educationincanada-eng.png/330px-Educationincanada-eng.png)](/wiki/File:Educationincanada-eng.png)

Canada by province and territory, showing the percentage of the population aged 25 to 64 who had a bachelor's degree or higher, and the percentage point change from 2016 to 2021[[350]](#cite_note-Statistics_Canada_2022_i032-357)

Education in Canada is for the most part [provided publicly](/wiki/State_school "State school"), funded and overseen by [federal](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada"), [provincial](/wiki/Provinces_and_territories_of_Canada "Provinces and territories of Canada"), and [local governments](/wiki/Local_government "Local government").[[351]](#cite_note-358) Education is within provincial jurisdiction and a province's curriculum is overseen by its government.[[352]](#cite_note-359) Education in Canada is generally divided into [primary education](/wiki/Primary_education "Primary education"), followed by [secondary](/wiki/Secondary_education "Secondary education") and [post-secondary education](/wiki/Tertiary_education "Tertiary education"). Education in both English and French is available in most places across Canada.[[353]](#cite_note-360) Canada has a large number of universities, almost all of which are publicly funded.[[354]](#cite_note-361) Established in 1663, [Université Laval](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_Laval "Université Laval") is the oldest post-secondary institution in Canada.[[355]](#cite_note-BlakeKeshen2017p249-362) The nation's three top ranking universities are the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto"), [McGill](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University"), and the [University of British Columbia](/wiki/University_of_British_Columbia "University of British Columbia").[[356]](#cite_note-363) The largest university is the University of Toronto, which has over 85,000 students.[[357]](#cite_note-364)

According to a 2022 report by the OECD, Canada is one of the most educated countries in the world;[[358]](#cite_note-365) the country ranks first worldwide in the percentage of adults having tertiary education, with over 56 percent of Canadian adults having attained at least an undergraduate college or university degree.[[359]](#cite_note-Education_c106-366) Canada spends an average of 5.3 percent of its GDP on education.[[360]](#cite_note-TheGlobalEconomy.com_1971_w626-367) The country invests heavily in tertiary education (more than US$20,000 per student).[[361]](#cite_note-368) As of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Canada&action=edit), 89 percent of adults aged 25 to 64 have earned the equivalent of a high-school degree, compared to an OECD average of 75 percent.[[362]](#cite_note-OECD_2023_t514-369)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Canada](/wiki/Culture_of_Canada "Culture of Canada")

Further information: [Canadian values](/wiki/Canadian_values "Canadian values") and [Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/%22Multiculturalism%22_by_Francesco_Pirelli_%281985%29%2C_Union_Station%2C_Toronto%2C_Ontario%2C_2025-08-25.jpg/250px-%22Multiculturalism%22_by_Francesco_Pirelli_%281985%29%2C_Union_Station%2C_Toronto%2C_Ontario%2C_2025-08-25.jpg)](/wiki/File:%22Multiculturalism%22_by_Francesco_Pirelli_(1985),_Union_Station,_Toronto,_Ontario,_2025-08-25.jpg)

*[Monument to Multiculturalism](/wiki/Monument_to_Multiculturalism "Monument to Multiculturalism")* by Francesco Pirelli, in Toronto[[363]](#cite_note-Kuitenbrouwer_2010_g035-370)

Canada is influenced by [British](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom"), [French](/wiki/French_culture "French culture") and Indigenous cultures and traditions. During the 20th century, Canadians with African, Caribbean, and Asian heritages have added to [Canadian identity](/wiki/Canadian_identity "Canadian identity").[[364]](#cite_note-371) Canada's culture draws influences from its broad range of constituent nationalities, and policies that promote a [just society](/wiki/Just_society "Just society") are constitutionally protected.[[365]](#cite_note-372) Since the 1960s, Canada has emphasized [human rights](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Canada "Human rights in Canada") and inclusiveness for all its people.[[366]](#cite_note-373) Canadian identity shifted from primarily British-based to multicultural between the 1960s and 1970s.[[367]](#cite_note-d827-374)

[The official state policy of multiculturalism](/wiki/Multiculturalism_in_Canada "Multiculturalism in Canada") is often cited as one of Canada's significant accomplishments[[368]](#cite_note-375) and a key distinguishing element of Canadian identity.[[369]](#cite_note-376) In Quebec, cultural identity is strong and there is a [French Canadian culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Quebec "Culture of Quebec") that is distinct from [English Canadian](/wiki/English_Canada "English Canada") culture.[[370]](#cite_note-377) As a whole, Canada is in theory a [cultural mosaic](/wiki/Cultural_mosaic "Cultural mosaic") of regional ethnic subcultures with diverse areas and [ethnic enclaves](/wiki/Multiculturalism_in_Canada#Settlement "Multiculturalism in Canada").[[371]](#cite_note-378)

Canada's approach to governance emphasizing multiculturalism, which is based on selective [immigration](/wiki/Economic_migrant "Economic migrant"), [social integration](/wiki/Social_integration "Social integration"), and [suppression](/wiki/Dissent "Dissent") of far-right politics, has wide public support.[[372]](#cite_note-p671-379) Government policies such as publicly funded health care, [higher taxation](/wiki/Income_taxes_in_Canada "Income taxes in Canada") to redistribute wealth, the [outlawing of capital punishment](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_Canada "Capital punishment in Canada"), strong efforts to [eliminate poverty](/wiki/Poverty_in_Canada "Poverty in Canada"), [strict gun control](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_Canada "Gun politics in Canada"), a [social liberal](/wiki/Social_liberal "Social liberal") attitude toward [women's rights](/wiki/Feminism_in_Canada "Feminism in Canada") (like [pregnancy termination](/wiki/Abortion_in_Canada "Abortion in Canada")) and [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Canada "LGBT rights in Canada"), and legalized [euthanasia](/wiki/Euthanasia_in_Canada "Euthanasia in Canada") and [cannabis use](/wiki/Cannabis_in_Canada "Cannabis in Canada") are indicators of Canada's political and cultural values.[[373]](#cite_note-380) Canadians also identify with the country's foreign aid policies, peacekeeping roles, the [national park system](/wiki/National_Parks_of_Canada "National Parks of Canada"), and the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.[[374]](#cite_note-381)

### Symbols

Main articles: [National symbols of Canada](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Canada "National symbols of Canada") and [Canadian royal symbols](/wiki/Canadian_royal_symbols "Canadian royal symbols")

[![The Mother Beaver sculpture outside the House of Commons](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Beaver_sculpture%2C_Centre_Block.jpg/250px-Beaver_sculpture%2C_Centre_Block.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beaver_sculpture,_Centre_Block.jpg)

*The Mother Beaver* on the Canadian parliament's [Peace Tower](/wiki/Peace_Tower "Peace Tower").[[375]](#cite_note-382) The five flowers on the shield each represent an ethnicity—[Tudor rose](/wiki/Tudor_rose "Tudor rose"): [English](/wiki/English_people "English people"); [fleur de lis](/wiki/Fleur_de_lis "Fleur de lis"): [French](/wiki/French_people "French people"); [thistle](/wiki/Thistle "Thistle"): [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_people "Scottish people"); [shamrock](/wiki/Shamrock "Shamrock"): [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people"); and [leek](/wiki/Leek "Leek"): [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_people "Welsh people").

Themes of nature, pioneers, trappers, and traders played an important part in the early development of Canadian symbolism.[[376]](#cite_note-383) Modern symbols emphasize the country's geography, northern climate, lifestyles, and the Canadianization of traditional European and Indigenous symbols.[[377]](#cite_note-384) The use of the [maple leaf](/wiki/Maple_leaf "Maple leaf") as a symbol dates to the early 18th century in New France.[[378]](#cite_note-g606-385) The maple leaf is depicted on Canada's [current](/wiki/Flag_of_Canada "Flag of Canada") and [previous flags](/wiki/Canadian_Red_Ensign "Canadian Red Ensign") and on the [arms of Canada](/wiki/Arms_of_Canada "Arms of Canada").[[379]](#cite_note-symbol1-386) Canada's official tartan, known as the "[maple leaf tartan](/wiki/Regional_tartans_of_Canada "Regional tartans of Canada")", reflects the colours of the maple leaf through the seasons—green in the [spring](/wiki/Spring_(season) "Spring (season)"), gold in the early [autumn](/wiki/Autumn "Autumn"), red at the first [frost](/wiki/Frost "Frost"), and brown after falling.[[380]](#cite_note-387) The arms of Canada are closely modelled after [those of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Royal_coat_of_arms_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal coat of arms of the United Kingdom"), with French and distinctive Canadian elements replacing or added to those derived from the British version.[[381]](#cite_note-388)

Other prominent symbols include the national motto, "*[A mari usque ad mare](/wiki/A_mari_usque_ad_mare "A mari usque ad mare")*" ("from sea to sea"),[[382]](#cite_note-389) the sports of [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") and [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse "Lacrosse"), the [beaver](/wiki/Beaver "Beaver"), [Canada goose](/wiki/Canada_goose "Canada goose"), [common loon](/wiki/Common_loon "Common loon"), [Canadian horse](/wiki/Canadian_horse "Canadian horse"), the Royal Canadian Mounted Police, the Canadian Rockies,[[379]](#cite_note-symbol1-386) and, more recently, the Indigenous [totem pole](/wiki/Totem_pole "Totem pole") and [Inuksuk](/wiki/Inuksuk "Inuksuk").[[383]](#cite_note-Nels-390) [Canadian cuisine](/wiki/Canadian_cuisine "Canadian cuisine") items such as [Canadian beer](/wiki/Canadian_beer "Canadian beer"), [maple syrup](/wiki/Maple_syrup "Maple syrup"), [Nanaimo bars](/wiki/Nanaimo_bar "Nanaimo bar"), [butter tarts](/wiki/Butter_tart "Butter tart"), and the [Quebec dishes](/wiki/Cuisine_of_Quebec "Cuisine of Quebec") of [poutine](/wiki/Poutine "Poutine") and [tourtière](/wiki/Tourti%C3%A8re "Tourtière"), alongside material items such as [tuques](/wiki/Tuque "Tuque"), [canoes](/wiki/Canoes "Canoes") and [Hudson's Bay point blanket](/wiki/Hudson%27s_Bay_point_blanket "Hudson's Bay point blanket") are considered as uniquely Canadian.[[384]](#cite_note-391) Canadian coins feature many of these symbols: the loon on the [$1 coin](/wiki/Loonie "Loonie"), the coat of arms on the [50¢ piece](/wiki/50-cent_piece_(Canadian_coin) "50-cent piece (Canadian coin)"), and the beaver on the [nickel](/wiki/Nickel_(Canadian_coin) "Nickel (Canadian coin)").[[385]](#cite_note-Berman2008-392) An image of the monarch appears on [$20 bank notes](/wiki/Canadian_dollar "Canadian dollar") and the obverse of coins.[[385]](#cite_note-Berman2008-392)

### Literature

Main article: [Canadian literature](/wiki/Canadian_literature "Canadian literature")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Margaret_Atwood_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg/250px-Margaret_Atwood_%283x4_cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Margaret_Atwood_(3x4_cropped).jpg)

Author [Margaret Atwood](/wiki/Margaret_Atwood "Margaret Atwood") has suggested that during the 1970s Canadian literature was still looking for a national identity.[[386]](#cite_note-n066-393)

Canadian literature is often divided into French- and English-language literatures, which are rooted in the literary traditions of France and Britain, respectively.[[387]](#cite_note-394) The earliest Canadian narratives were of travel and exploration.[[388]](#cite_note-395) This developed into three major themes of historical Canadian literature: nature, frontier life, and Canada's position within the world, all of which tie into the [garrison mentality](/wiki/Garrison_mentality "Garrison mentality").[[389]](#cite_note-396) The evolution of Canadian literature is intricately linked to the country's historical and social contexts, often mirroring the challenges and changes in Canadian society.[[390]](#cite_note-u778-397) As Canadian literature progressed into the 20th and 21st centuries, it began to address a broader array of subjects and themes, such as women's rights, LGBTQ rights, immigrant experiences, environmental issues, the relationship with Indigenous peoples, and Canadian values and identity.[[391]](#cite_note-h220-398)

Financial support from governmental bodies, such as the [Canada Council for the Arts](/wiki/Canada_Council_for_the_Arts "Canada Council for the Arts") and various provincial grant programs, facilitates the creation, publication, and promotion of works by [Canadian authors](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_writers "List of Canadian writers").[[392]](#cite_note-p619-399) Numerous [Canadian authors have received international literary awards](/wiki/Canadian_literature#Canadian_authors_who_have_won_international_awards "Canadian literature") including the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature"), the [Booker Prize](/wiki/Booker_Prize "Booker Prize"), and the [Pulitzer Prize for Fiction](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Fiction "Pulitzer Prize for Fiction").[[393]](#cite_note-400) [Canadian literary awards and prizes](/wiki/Canadian_literature#Awards "Canadian literature") include the [Governor General's Literary Awards](/wiki/Governor_General%27s_Literary_Awards "Governor General's Literary Awards"), the [Giller Prize](/wiki/Giller_Prize "Giller Prize"), the [Latner Griffin Writers' Trust Poetry Prize](/wiki/Latner_Griffin_Writers%27_Trust_Poetry_Prize "Latner Griffin Writers' Trust Poetry Prize"), the [Burt Award for First Nations, Inuit and Métis Literature](/wiki/Burt_Award_for_First_Nations,_Inuit_and_M%C3%A9tis_Literature "Burt Award for First Nations, Inuit and Métis Literature") and several accolades for literature aimed at children.[[394]](#cite_note-i923-401)

### Visual arts

Main article: [Canadian art](/wiki/Canadian_art "Canadian art")

[![Oil on canvas painting of a tree dominating its rocky landscape during a sunset](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/The_Jack_Pine%2C_by_Tom_Thomson.jpg/250px-The_Jack_Pine%2C_by_Tom_Thomson.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Jack_Pine,_by_Tom_Thomson.jpg)

*[The Jack Pine](/wiki/The_Jack_Pine "The Jack Pine")* by [Tom Thomson](/wiki/Tom_Thomson "Tom Thomson"). Oil on canvas, 1916, in the collection of the [National Gallery of Canada](/wiki/National_Gallery_of_Canada "National Gallery of Canada").[[395]](#cite_note-Art_Canada_Institute_-_Institut_de_l'art_canadien_h165-402)

Art in Canada is marked by thousands of years of habitation by Indigenous peoples,[[396]](#cite_note-403) and, in later times, artists have combined British, French, Indigenous, and American artistic traditions, at times embracing European styles while working to promote nationalism.[[397]](#cite_note-404) The nature of Canadian art reflects these diverse origins, as artists have taken their traditions and adapted these influences to reflect the reality of their lives in Canada.[[398]](#cite_note-405) The [Group of Seven](/wiki/Group_of_Seven_(artists) "Group of Seven (artists)") is often considered the first uniquely Canadian artistic group and style of painting.[[399]](#cite_note-Jessup2001-406) [Inuit art](/wiki/Inuit_art "Inuit art") since the 1950s has been the traditional gift given to foreign dignitaries by the Canadian government.[[400]](#cite_note-Stern2010-407)

Historically, the Catholic Church was the primary patron of art in early Canada, especially Quebec.[[401]](#cite_note-j452-408) The Government of Canada has played a role in the development of art, through the department of [Canadian Heritage](/wiki/Canadian_Heritage "Canadian Heritage") by giving grants to art galleries,[[402]](#cite_note-409) as well as by establishing and funding art schools and colleges across the country, and through the Canada Council for the Arts.[[403]](#cite_note-e229-410) The Canada Council [Art Bank](/wiki/Art_Bank "Art Bank") also helps artists by buying and publicizing their work.[[404]](#cite_note-j314-411) Great achievements in art in Canada are recognized through [various awards and prizes](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_art_awards "List of Canadian art awards"), such as the [Molson Prize](/wiki/Molson_Prize "Molson Prize"), the [Audain Prize for the Visual Arts](/wiki/Audain_Prize_for_the_Visual_Arts "Audain Prize for the Visual Arts"), and the [Governor General's Visual and Media Arts Awards](/wiki/List_of_recipients_of_the_Governor_General%27s_Award_in_Visual_and_Media_Arts "List of recipients of the Governor General's Award in Visual and Media Arts").[[405]](#cite_note-n795-412)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Canada](/wiki/Music_of_Canada "Music of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/O-Canada-1908.pdf/page1-250px-O-Canada-1908.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:O-Canada-1908.pdf)

Original publication of "[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")" in English, 1908 (French, 1880)[[406]](#cite_note-The_Canadian_Encyclopedia_2018_g033-413)

Canadian music reflects a [variety of regional scenes](/wiki/Music_of_Canadian_cultures "Music of Canadian cultures").[[407]](#cite_note-414) Canada has developed a vast music infrastructure that includes [church halls](/wiki/Church_hall "Church hall"), [chamber halls](/wiki/Chamber_music "Chamber music"), [conservatories](/wiki/Music_school "Music school"), [academies](/wiki/Academies "Academies"), [performing arts centres](/wiki/Performing_arts_center "Performing arts center"), [record companies](/wiki/Record_company "Record company"), [radio stations](/wiki/Radio_stations "Radio stations"), [music charts](/wiki/Canadian_music_charts "Canadian music charts"), and television [music video](/wiki/Music_video "Music video") channels.[[408]](#cite_note-415) Government support programs, such as the Canada Music Fund, assist a wide range of musicians and entrepreneurs who create, produce and market original and diverse Canadian music.[[409]](#cite_note-416) As a result of its cultural importance, as well as government initiatives and regulations, the Canadian music industry is one of the largest in the world,[[410]](#cite_note-417) producing internationally renowned [composers](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_composers "List of Canadian composers"), [musicians](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_musicians "List of Canadian musicians"), and [ensembles](/wiki/List_of_bands_from_Canada "List of bands from Canada").[[411]](#cite_note-418) Music broadcasting in the country is regulated by the CRTC.[[412]](#cite_note-419) The [Canadian Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences](/wiki/Canadian_Academy_of_Recording_Arts_and_Sciences "Canadian Academy of Recording Arts and Sciences") presents Canada's music industry awards, the [Juno Awards](/wiki/Juno_Award "Juno Award").[[413]](#cite_note-Edwardson,_Ryan-420): [127](https://archive.org/details/canadiancontentc0000edwa/page/127) The [Canadian Music Hall of Fame](/wiki/Canadian_Music_Hall_of_Fame "Canadian Music Hall of Fame") honours Canadian musicians for their lifetime achievements.[[414]](#cite_note-421)

"[God Save the King](/wiki/God_Save_the_King "God Save the King")" has been used in Canada since the late 1700s and is the country's de facto royal anthem.[[415]](#cite_note-t663-422) [Patriotic music by Canadians](/wiki/Anthems_and_nationalistic_songs_of_Canada "Anthems and nationalistic songs of Canada") dates back over 200 years, with "[The Bold Canadian](/wiki/The_Bold_Canadian "The Bold Canadian")", written in 1812, popular throughout the 19th century.[[416]](#cite_note-423) "[The Maple Leaf Forever](/wiki/The_Maple_Leaf_Forever "The Maple Leaf Forever")", written in 1866, was popular and served as an unofficial national anthem of English Canada.[[417]](#cite_note-424) "[O Canada](/wiki/O_Canada "O Canada")", originally composed in French in 1880, also served as an unofficial national anthem during the 20th century and was adopted as the country's official anthem in 1980.[[406]](#cite_note-The_Canadian_Encyclopedia_2018_g033-413)

### Media

Main article: [Media of Canada](/wiki/Media_of_Canada "Media of Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/CBC_Radio_Canada_Chevrolet_Express_02.jpg/250px-CBC_Radio_Canada_Chevrolet_Express_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:CBC_Radio_Canada_Chevrolet_Express_02.jpg)

A [Canadian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") (CBC) satellite truck, used for live television broadcasts

Canada's media is [highly autonomous](/wiki/Press_Freedom_Index "Press Freedom Index"), [uncensored](/wiki/Censorship_by_country "Censorship by country"), [diverse](/wiki/Multicultural_media_in_Canada "Multicultural media in Canada"), and very regionalized.[[418]](#cite_note-425) The *[Broadcasting Act](/wiki/Broadcasting_Act_(1991) "Broadcasting Act (1991)")* declares "the system should serve to safeguard, enrich, and strengthen the cultural, political, social, and economic fabric of Canada".[[419]](#cite_note-426) Canada has a well-developed media sector, but its cultural output—particularly in [English films](/wiki/Cinema_of_Canada "Cinema of Canada"), [television shows](/wiki/Television_in_Canada "Television in Canada"), and [magazines](/wiki/List_of_Canadian_magazines "List of Canadian magazines")—is often overshadowed by imports from the United States.[[420]](#cite_note-427) As a result, the preservation of a distinctly Canadian culture is supported by federal government programs, laws, and institutions such as the [Canadian Broadcasting Corporation](/wiki/Canadian_Broadcasting_Corporation "Canadian Broadcasting Corporation") (CBC), the [National Film Board of Canada](/wiki/National_Film_Board_of_Canada "National Film Board of Canada") (NFB), and the [Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission](/wiki/Canadian_Radio-television_and_Telecommunications_Commission "Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission") (CRTC).[[413]](#cite_note-Edwardson,_Ryan-420): [59](https://archive.org/details/canadiancontentc0000edwa/page/59)

[Canadian mass media](/wiki/Mass_media_in_Canada "Mass media in Canada"), both [print](/wiki/Newspapers_of_Canada "Newspapers of Canada") and [digital](/wiki/Canadian_online_media "Canadian online media"), and in both official languages, is largely dominated by a "[handful of corporations](/wiki/Media_ownership_in_Canada "Media ownership in Canada")".[[421]](#cite_note-TarasBakardjievaPannekoek20072-428) The largest of these corporations is the country's national [public broadcaster](/wiki/Public_broadcaster "Public broadcaster"), the Canadian Broadcasting Corporation, which also plays a significant role in producing domestic cultural content, operating [its own radio](/wiki/CBC_Radio "CBC Radio") and [TV](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television") networks in both English and French.[[422]](#cite_note-TarasBakardjievaPannekoek2007-429) In addition to the CBC, some provincial governments offer their own public educational TV broadcast services as well, such as [TVOntario](/wiki/TVOntario "TVOntario") and [Télé-Québec](/wiki/T%C3%A9l%C3%A9-Qu%C3%A9bec "Télé-Québec").[[423]](#cite_note-430)

Non-news media content in Canada, including film and television, is influenced both by local creators as well as by imports from the United States, the United Kingdom, Australia, and France.[[424]](#cite_note-431) In an effort to reduce the amount of foreign-made media, government interventions in television broadcasting can include both regulation of content and public financing.[[425]](#cite_note-432) [Canadian tax laws](/wiki/Taxation_in_Canada "Taxation in Canada") limit foreign competition in magazine advertising.[[426]](#cite_note-433)

### Sports

Main article: [Sports in Canada](/wiki/Sports_in_Canada "Sports in Canada")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame_Statue_%289162222032%29.jpg/250px-Hockey_Hall_of_Fame_Statue_%289162222032%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hockey_Hall_of_Fame_Statue_(9162222032).jpg)

*Our Game* sculpture by [Edie Parker](/wiki/Edie_Parker_(artist) "Edie Parker (artist)") outside the [Hockey Hall of Fame](/wiki/Hockey_Hall_of_Fame "Hockey Hall of Fame") in Toronto[[427]](#cite_note-k228-434)

Canada's official national sports are [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey_in_Canada "Ice hockey in Canada") and [lacrosse](/wiki/Lacrosse_in_Canada "Lacrosse in Canada").[[428]](#cite_note-435) Other major professional games include [curling](/wiki/Curling "Curling"), [basketball](/wiki/Basketball_in_Canada "Basketball in Canada"), [baseball](/wiki/Baseball_in_Canada "Baseball in Canada"), [soccer](/wiki/Soccer_in_Canada "Soccer in Canada"), and [football](/wiki/Canadian_football "Canadian football").[[429]](#cite_note-436) Great achievements in Canadian sports are recognized by numerous "Halls of Fame" and museums, such as [Canada's Sports Hall of Fame](/wiki/Canada%27s_Sports_Hall_of_Fame "Canada's Sports Hall of Fame").[[430]](#cite_note-Danilov1997cv-437)

Canada shares several [major professional sports leagues](/wiki/Major_professional_sports_leagues_in_the_United_States_and_Canada "Major professional sports leagues in the United States and Canada") with the United States.[[431]](#cite_note-438) Canadian teams in these leagues include seven franchises in the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"), three [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Soccer_in_Canada#Major_League_Soccer "Soccer in Canada") teams, and one team in each of [Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball") and the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"). Other popular professional competitions include the [Canadian Football League](/wiki/Canadian_Football_League "Canadian Football League"), [National Lacrosse League](/wiki/National_Lacrosse_League "National Lacrosse League"), the [Canadian Premier League](/wiki/Canadian_Premier_League "Canadian Premier League"), and the curling tournaments hosted by [Curling Canada](/wiki/Curling_Canada "Curling Canada").[[432]](#cite_note-439) Canadians identified hockey as their preferred sport for viewing, followed by soccer and then basketball.[[433]](#cite_note-n113-440)

In terms of participation, [swimming](/wiki/Swimming "Swimming") was the most commonly reported sport by over one-third (35 percent) of Canadians in 2023.[[434]](#cite_note-h903-441) This was closely followed by [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling") (33 percent) and [running](/wiki/Running "Running") (27 percent).[[434]](#cite_note-h903-441) The popularity of specific sports varies;[[435]](#cite_note-442) in general, the Canadian-born population was more likely to have participated in [winter sports](/wiki/Winter_sports "Winter sports") such as ice hockey (the most popular young adult team sport),[[434]](#cite_note-h903-441) [skating](/wiki/Ice_skating "Ice skating"), [skiing](/wiki/Skiing "Skiing") and [snowboarding](/wiki/Snowboarding "Snowboarding"), compared with immigrants, who were more likely to have played soccer (the most popular youth team sport),[[436]](#cite_note-a691-443) [tennis](/wiki/Tennis "Tennis") or basketball.[[434]](#cite_note-h903-441) Sports such as [golf](/wiki/Golf "Golf"), [volleyball](/wiki/Volleyball_in_Canada "Volleyball in Canada"), [badminton](/wiki/Badminton "Badminton"), [bowling](/wiki/Bowling "Bowling"), and [martial arts](/wiki/Martial_arts "Martial arts") are also widely enjoyed at the youth and amateur levels.[[437]](#cite_note-444)

Canada has enjoyed success both [at the Winter Olympics](/wiki/Canada_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Canada at the Winter Olympics") and [at the Summer Olympics](/wiki/Canada_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Canada at the Summer Olympics")[[438]](#cite_note-445)—particularly the Winter Games as a "winter sports nation"—and has hosted high-profile international sporting events such as the [1976 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1976_Summer_Olympics "1976 Summer Olympics"),[[439]](#cite_note-446) the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics"),[[440]](#cite_note-447) the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics"),[[441]](#cite_note-448) the [2015 FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/2015_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "2015 FIFA Women's World Cup"),[[442]](#cite_note-449) the [2015 Pan American Games](/wiki/2015_Pan_American_Games "2015 Pan American Games") and [2015 Parapan American Games](/wiki/2015_Parapan_American_Games "2015 Parapan American Games").[[443]](#cite_note-450) The country is scheduled to co-host the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup") alongside Mexico and the United States.[[444]](#cite_note-451)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** English: [/ˈkænədə/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:En-ca-Canada.ogg "File:En-ca-Canada.ogg"),[[8]](#cite_note-j933-8) French: [[kanada]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Fr-Canada.ogg "File:Fr-Canada.ogg")
2. **[^](#cite_ref-canadausa_123-0)** 6,416 km (3,987 mi) via the [contiguous 48 states](/wiki/Contiguous_48_states "Contiguous 48 states") and 2,475 km (1,538 mi) via [Alaska](/wiki/Alaska "Alaska")[[121]](#cite_note-International_Boundary_Commission-122)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-politics_161-0)** "Brokerage politics: A Canadian term for successful [big tent parties](/wiki/Big_tent "Big tent") that embody a [pluralistic](/wiki/Pluralism_(political_philosophy) "Pluralism (political philosophy)") catch-all approach to appeal to the median Canadian voter ... adopting [centrist policies](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") and [electoral coalitions](/wiki/Electoral_alliance "Electoral alliance") to satisfy the short-term preferences of a majority of electors who are not located on the ideological fringe."[[157]](#cite_note-159) "The traditional *brokerage* model of Canadian politics leaves little room for ideology."[[158]](#cite_note-160)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-Force_224-0)**  "The [Royal Canadian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Canadian_Navy "Royal Canadian Navy") is composed of approximately 8,400 full-time sailors and 5,100 part-time sailors. The Army is composed of approximately 22,800 full-time soldiers, 18,700 reservists, and 5,000 [Canadian Rangers](/wiki/Canadian_Rangers "Canadian Rangers"). The Royal Canadian Air Force is composed of approximately 13,000 Regular Force personnel and 2,400 Air Reserve personnel."[[220]](#cite_note-223)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-Canadian_304-0)** All citizens of Canada are classified as "Canadians" as defined by [Canada's nationality laws](/wiki/Canadian_nationality_law "Canadian nationality law"). "Canadian" as an ethnic group has since 1996 been added to census questionnaires for possible ancestral origin or descent. "Canadian" was included as an example on the English questionnaire and "Canadien" as an example on the French questionnaire.[[298]](#cite_note-302) "The majority of respondents to this selection are from the eastern part of the country that was first settled. Respondents generally are visibly European (Anglophones and Francophones) and no longer self-identify with their ethnic ancestral origins. This response is attributed to a multitude or generational distance from ancestral lineage."[[299]](#cite_note-303)
6. **[^](#cite_ref-309)** Indigenous peoples are not considered a visible minority in Statistics Canada calculations. Visible minorities are defined by Statistics Canada as "persons, other than Aboriginal peoples, who are non-Caucasian in race or non-white in colour".[[303]](#cite_note-308)
7. **[^](#cite_ref-religion_338-0)** [Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Canada "Roman Catholicism in Canada") (29.9%), [United Church](/wiki/United_Church_of_Canada "United Church of Canada") (3.3%), [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Church_of_Canada "Anglican Church of Canada") (3.1%), [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") (1.7%), [Baptistism](/wiki/Baptists_in_Canada "Baptists in Canada") (1.2%), [Pentecostalism and other Charismatic](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") (1.1%) [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism") (0.4%), [Jehovah's Witness](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") (0.4%), [Latter Day Saints](/wiki/Latter_Day_Saint_movement "Latter Day Saint movement") (0.2%), [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") (0.9%), [Methodist](/wiki/Methodism "Methodism") and [Wesleyan](/wiki/Wesleyan_Church "Wesleyan Church") (Holiness) (0.3%), [Presbyterian](/wiki/Presbyterianism "Presbyterianism") (0.8%), and [Reformed](/wiki/Reformed_Episcopal_Church "Reformed Episcopal Church") (0.2%).[[330]](#cite_note-religion2021-336) 7.6 percent simply identified as "Christians".[[331]](#cite_note-The_Canadian_Encyclopedia_2022_z483-337)
